_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Davidson County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Davidson County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Lexington, NC — 110 W. Center Street (extensively renovated multiple times; one of the oldest active courthouses in NC)

previous_courthouses.fate: First courthouse built when Davidson County was formed from Rowan County in 1822. Replaced by the 1858 building which still stands.

record_losses.cause: None — no major courthouse fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Davidson County was formed from Rowan County in 1822 and has suffered no major courthouse fire or war destruction. The 1858 courthouse itself survives — one of the oldest active courthouse buildings in North Carolina. Records begin at the county's formation date and run continuously. For pre-1822 records, Rowan County (Salisbury) is the parent county and holds the earlier series.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Davidson County's records are complete from 1822, but {ancestor_name}'s family may have been on this land for decades before the county was formed. Rowan County records at Salisbury cover the pre-1822 period and extend back to 1753.
record_losses.source: Sink, Margaret Jewell, and Mary Green Matthews. Pathfinders Past and Present: A History of Davidson County, North Carolina. High Point: Hall Printing, 1972.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1822 deeds for land within current Davidson County boundaries are in Rowan County records at Salisbury.
deeds.repository: Davidson County Register of Deeds, Lexington; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from formation
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1822 wills are in Rowan County records.
wills_estates.repository: Davidson County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.gaps: Pre-1822 marriages are in Rowan County records.
marriages.repository: Davidson County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Davidson County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for the territory that became Davidson County are found in Rowan County (Salisbury) before 1822. Rowan County was formed in 1753 and has excellent record survival — it is one of the oldest and most genealogically important counties in western North Carolina. The German and Scots-Irish families who settled Davidson County territory appear in Rowan County deeds, wills, court minutes, and tax lists back to the colonial period.
substitute_sources.repository: Rowan County Register of Deeds, Salisbury; NC State Archives microfilm; FamilySearch
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Davidson County family research before 1822. Rowan County's colonial-era records are extensive and well-indexed, providing a rich trail for families that were on Davidson County land before the county existed.
substitute_sources.source: Brawley, James S. The Rowan Story, 1753-1953: A Narrative History of Rowan County, North Carolina. Salisbury: Rowan Printing, 1953.

substitute_sources.source_type: German settlement church records — Lutheran and Reformed
substitute_sources.description: Davidson County was settled heavily by German-speaking families in the mid-to-late eighteenth century. Lutheran and German Reformed (later UCC) congregations — including Jersey Baptist Church (misnamed; originally German Reformed), Beck's Reformed Church, and Pilgrim Reformed Church — kept baptismal, marriage, and burial records, some in German, from the colonial period forward.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual churches; NC State Archives (some microfilmed); Evangelical and Reformed Historical Society, Lancaster, PA; Concordia Historical Institute, St. Louis (Lutheran records)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for German-descent families. Church records often provide baptismal sponsors' names — the sponsors were typically family members, creating a web of kinship documentation not available in civil records. Records in German before approximately 1830-1850 require translation but contain detailed family information.
substitute_sources.source: Bernheim, G. D. History of the German Settlements and of the Lutheran Church in North and South Carolina. Philadelphia: Lutheran Book Store, 1872.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Before 1830, families in present-day Davidson County territory were enumerated under Rowan County. From 1830 onward, under Davidson County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families across the 1822 county formation. The 1830 census is the first to enumerate Davidson County separately.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Davidson and Rowan Counties, NC.

substitute_sources.description: Thomasville and Lexington were major furniture manufacturing centers from the late nineteenth century. Some company employment records survive and document the workforce that defined Davidson County's economy.
substitute_sources.repository: Davidson County Historical Museum, Lexington; NC State Archives; individual company archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Useful for identifying furniture worker families and tracking employment dates. Less complete than the textile mill archives in neighboring counties but valuable when available.
substitute_sources.source: Lefler, Hugh Talmage, and Albert Ray Newsome. North Carolina: The History of a Southern State. 3rd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1973.

narrative_hooks: Davidson County's courthouse — the same 1858 building — still stands and still holds the records. If {ancestor_name} bought land here after 1822, the deed is almost certainly in the same building where it was first recorded.

If {ancestor_name}'s family was on Davidson County land before 1822, the paper trail leads to Rowan County at Salisbury — one of the oldest and richest record repositories in western North Carolina, with colonial records back to 1753.

If {ancestor_name} was of German descent in Davidson County, the Lutheran or Reformed church records may document {pronoun_possessive} family in German — baptismal sponsors, marriage witnesses, burial entries in the old language that trace connections back to the first settlers.

Before 1913, no North Carolina county required birth or death registration, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} in Davidson County is expected — German church records, census schedules, and Rowan County parent records fill the gap.
source_references: Sink, Margaret Jewell, and Mary Green Matthews. Pathfinders Past and Present: A History of Davidson County, North Carolina. High Point: Hall Printing, 1972.
Brawley, James S. The Rowan Story, 1753-1953: A Narrative History of Rowan County, North Carolina. Salisbury: Rowan Printing, 1953.
Bernheim, G. D. History of the German Settlements and of the Lutheran Church in North and South Carolina. Philadelphia: Lutheran Book Store, 1872.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: North Carolina County Records. Raleigh, 1937–1942.
